title: Spaghetti alle cozze
subtitle: Mussels, garlic, white wine, and parsley
excerpt: Mussels steamed open in white wine and garlic, their briny liquid pulled into the pan with the pasta and a handful of flat-leaf parsley. A Neapolitan Friday-night dish that reads far more impressive than the time it takes.
sourceNotes: Pasta con le cozze is a canonical Neapolitan preparation, documented in regional cookbooks throughout the nineteenth century. Artusi includes a version of pasta with shellfish juice in La scienza in cucina (1891, public domain). The pairing of mussels, white wine, and parsley over spaghetti is considered by Neapolitan cooks a non-negotiable combination.

content.text: The mussel liquid is the sauce. That is the point of this recipe: the shells open and release a concentrated briny stock into the wine and garlic, and that liquid carries the flavour of the sea into every strand of pasta. You are not making a sauce to add to mussels; you are harvesting what the mussels produce.

Buy the mussels on the day you plan to cook them. Clean them thoroughly: pull off the beard (the fibrous threads at the hinge) and scrub the shells. Discard any that are open and do not close when tapped sharply on the side of the sink.

items.name: Mussels
items.prepNote: scrubbed, beards removed, any open ones discarded

content.text: Heat 2 tablespoons of the olive oil in a large, deep pan over high heat. Add half the garlic and cook for 30 seconds. Add the {{mussels}} of mussels and the {{white-wine-dry}} of white wine. Clamp on the lid. Cook for 3 to 4 minutes, shaking the pan once, until all the mussels have opened. Discard any that remain shut. Tip into a colander set over a large bowl to catch the liquid. Pick the mussels from the shells, leaving a few in the shell for presentation. Strain the liquid through a fine sieve if it looks gritty.
Bring a large pan of salted water to a boil. Cook the {{pasta-dried}} of spaghetti until 2 minutes short of the al dente time. Scoop out 100 ml of pasta water. Drain.

In the same large pan, heat the remaining 2 tablespoons of olive oil over medium heat. Add the rest of the garlic and the chilli flakes if using. Cook for 1 minute. Add the strained mussel liquid and bring to a simmer. Add the drained pasta and toss for 2 minutes until it absorbs most of the liquid. Add the mussels and the parsley, toss briefly, and squeeze over the lemon juice if using. The pasta should be glossy and the sauce barely there, just coating the strands.

items.fix: Watch the pan, the moment the last shell opens, remove from the heat immediately
items.cause: Overcooked during the steaming stage; mussels are done as soon as the shells open

items.fix: Always strain through a fine-mesh sieve or a muslin-lined colander
items.cause: Mussel liquid poured directly into the pan without straining

items.fix: The liquid is non-negotiable. Very fresh mussels give a much tastier liquid.
items.cause: Mussel liquid discarded, or mussels were not fresh

items.fix: Pull the pasta 2 minutes early; it finishes cooking in the pan and absorbs the sauce gradually
items.cause: Pasta cooked too long before going into the mussel liquid
items.symptom: Pasta absorbs all the sauce and the dish is dry

content.text: Spaghetti alle cozze is a Friday dish in Naples. Friday was a day to skip meat, which made seafood pasta a staple of the city's poorer streets, where mussels were cheap and plentiful. The Bay of Naples sits over volcanic rock that grows particularly tasty mussels. The city has always eaten its shellfish with intent. Raw with lemon at the street stalls near the port, and like this, cooked in their own steam over a tangle of spaghetti.
